summary: Gruber argues that Apple's rejection of the Podcaster app from the App Store on grounds of 'duplicating functionality' is a disastrous policy that forbids competition and discourages serious developers. He cites Fraser Speirs's argument that rejecting apps at the final stage destroys developer confidence and investment. Gruber contends that since Apple's App Store is the sole distribution channel for iPhone apps, its policies must be inclusive rather than exclusionary. He frames the 'no duplication' rule as explicitly anti-competitive, noting that competition by definition means doing the same thing better. He concludes that either this is Apple's actual policy (making it a platform disaster) or Podcaster's rejection proves the approval process is broken—either way, something is seriously wrong.
key_points: Apple rejected Podcaster for 'duplicating' iTunes podcast functionality, effectively forbidding competition with its own apps
Since Apple's App Store is the only distribution channel, exclusionary policies are unacceptable if Apple wants a robust developer ecosystem
Developers who invest serious time and resources face unacceptable risk when apps can be rejected for conceptual rather than technical reasons
Forbidding duplication of functionality is by definition forbidding competition, and Apple hasn't even disclosed which functionality is off-limits
The situation presents a lose-lose: either Apple's policy is anti-competitive by design, or the approval process is fundamentally broken
money_quotes: Let's be clear: forbidding 'duplication of functionality' is forbidding competition. The point of competition is to do the same thing, but better.
If good developers are afraid to write software for your platform, it is a problem.
No one benefits from such a policy, not even Apple.
If this is truly Apple's policy, it's a disaster for the platform. And if it's not Apple's policy, then Podcaster's exclusion is proof that the approval process is completely broken.
Either way, something is seriously wrong.
An iPhone where you can't compete against Apple's own apps isn't much better than last year's SDK-less iPhone but with games and to-do list apps.

If you only find out at the end of the development process that your app has been rejected — not for a technical problem that you can address but because Apple deems the entire concept to be out of bounds — then who is going to put serious time and talent into an iPhone app?
key_insight: When a platform's only app distribution channel forbids competition with its own apps without even disclosing the rules, it destroys the developer confidence essential to a thriving ecosystem.
